title: Nettle, herb profile
subtitle: Urtica dioica: the most nutritive wild herb in the British countryside
excerpt: A materia medica profile of stinging nettle leaf, its traditional actions, nutritive properties, therapeutic uses across immune support, women's health, and skin, and safety profile. One of the most versatile and underused herbs available, literally growing in every hedgerow.
sourceNotes: Maud Grieve, A Modern Herbal (1931), nettle entry. The 17th-century herbalist Nicholas Culpeper, The English Physician (1652). European Medicines Agency assessment of Urtica dioica folium (2011). Mittman P. (1990), a freeze-dried nettle pilot study for hay fever.

content.text: Stinging nettle is a perennial herb that grows across Britain and most of the temperate world. The sting comes from hollow silica hairs on the leaves and stems. Drying, cooking, or blanching takes the sting out. The dried leaf is the medicinal part. Nettle sits between food and medicine: cooks use it for soup and pesto, and weavers once used it as a textile fibre. As a
, its mineral load is strong: iron, calcium, magnesium, potassium, and silica in forms the body takes up well.

Nutritive tonic with strong mineral and vitamin content. Antihistamine, calming the response that makes hay fever sneezing. Astringent, tightening skin and the linings of the nose and throat. Haemostatic, slowing heavy bleeding, with a long record for heavy periods. Diuretic, mild, supportive of the kidneys. Old herbals list it for the same uses: kidney support, blood tonic, iron source, and hay fever.

Hay fever, taken as a daily tea right through the pollen season. A small clinical pilot found most users rated freeze-dried nettle helpful. Cycle support, drunk in the second half of the cycle for iron and minerals. Mild skin troubles, used cool as a topical compress to calm swelling. Tiredness and low iron, as a daily nutritive tonic. Nettle soup and pesto for general nutrition. Old country use also includes pressing fresh nettles on aching joints, a practice better discussed with a practitioner before trying.

Nettle's food value is well established. Lab and animal work supports the antihistamine action. Human trial evidence is limited to one small pilot for hay fever, encouraging but not conclusive. A modern European review confirms traditional use for joint and urinary support. Hay fever and cycle support sit in the traditional column, not the trial-evidence column.

Infusion: 5 g dried leaf per 250 ml water, steeped covered for 10 minutes. One to two cups daily for hay fever prevention and cycle support. Topical compress: 5 g dried leaf in 200 ml water, cooled, applied to skin irritation or eczema patches. Food: young leaves (spring, before flowering) blanched and used as spinach. Fresh nettles: always handle fresh nettles with gloves, the sting is painful and can persist for 30 minutes.

Dried nettle leaf at tea strength is well tolerated for regular long-term use. Warfarin: nettle holds vitamin K, so large daily amounts (more than two or three cups) could shift INR. Tell your prescriber if you plan a regular nettle habit on warfarin. Pregnancy: nettle tea is widely used as a nutritive tonic and is treated as safe at normal tea strength. No major drug interactions are reported at tea doses. Fresh nettles: wear gloves to gather. The sting is not dangerous but is sharp and can last up to half an hour.
